Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
No malicious code is present in the global scope or `pkgver()` of this PKGBUILD. The source fetches the official Bisq `.deb` and its `.asc` signature from `github.com/bisq-network/bisq/releases`, which is the legitimate upstream. Checksums are provided and only the `.asc` uses `SKIP`. No command substitutions, dangerous downloads, or obfuscated code exist at top level. `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` are not executed during the requested commands due to `--nobuild --noprepare`, so their content is out of scope for this gate. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` is safe.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code runs during source fetch or PKGBUILD sourcing.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No malicious code runs during source fetch or PKGBUILD sourcing.[/]

[0/1] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is a standard Arch User Repository (AUR) binary package script for Bisq. It downloads the official released `.deb` from the project's GitHub repository, verifies the PGP signature with maintainer-provided keys, and provides a SHA-512 checksum for integrity. The `prepare()` function extracts the `data.tar.xz` from the `.deb` archive—this is normal for repackaging `.deb` contents. The `package()` function copies the extracted `opt` directory into the package and creates symlinks for the binary, desktop entry, and icon. There are no unexpected network requests, obfuscated code, dangerous commands, or attempts to exfiltrate or modify system files beyond the package's own install locations. The use of `SKIP` for the signature checksum is standard practice. The script is fully consistent with legitimate AUR packaging conventions.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR binary package with no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is a standard Java security policy configuration (`default.policy`) that ships with the Azul Zulu JDK bundled in the bisq-bin package. It defines permissions for various JDK internal modules (e.g., `java.net.http`, `jdk.crypto.ec`, `jdk.zipfs`) and Azul-specific modules (`com.azul.crs.client`, `com.azul.tooling`). The grants are identical to those distributed by the upstream JDK vendor -- granting `AllPermission` to several modules and broad file/network access to the HTTP client is normal Java runtime behavior, not injection by the AUR package. No obfuscation, no execution of external code, no exfiltration, and no deviation from expected packaging practices are present. The Azul-specific grants (e.g., `FilePermission <<ALL FILES>>` for CRS) are part of the vendor's crash reporting and tooling, which are upstream product decisions, not a supply-chain attack.
</details>
<summary>
Standard Java policy file from upstream vendor; no malicious content.</summary>
</security_assessment>

Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/bisq-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-stwyclpk...

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard source download from the official GitHub releases page of the Bisq project, using a predictable URL based on the pkgver variable. The source array for x86_64 architecture fetches a .deb package and its corresponding PGP signature, with the latter marked to skip verification via 'SKIP' in sha512sums_x86_64—but this is only for the .asc file, which is expected when verifying signatures with external tools. There is no command substitution, dynamic fetching, or untrusted code execution at the top level or within `pkgver()`. The `pkgver` is hardcoded, so no runtime calculation is needed, and no code outside of the guarded `prepare()` and `package()` functions is executed during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`.

The commands `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will only source the PKGBUILD and download the listed sources. This involves a straightforward HTTPS download from github.com/bisq-network, which is the project's legitimate upstream. No data exfiltration, dynamic code evaluation, or suspicious network activity occurs during sourcing. The usage of SKIP for the .asc file's checksum does not imply insecurity in this context, as PGP signature verification is handled independently via `validpgpkeys`, and skipping the hash for detached signatures is a common practice.

No function or variable in the global scope performs side effects such as network calls, file uploads, or execution of fetched content. The operations that do occur—downloading a release binary and its signature—are safe and expected for AUR packaging. Therefore, the two specified commands pose no security risk during execution.
</details>
<summary>
Safe fetch and parse with no side effects
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe fetch and parse with no side effects[/]

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches a pre-built Debian package (.deb) from the official GitHub releases page of the Bisq project and verifies its PGP signature using two declared valid PGP keys. The `sha512sums` for the binary package is set to 'SKIP' for the .deb file, but the corresponding .asc signature file is expected and included, and PGP validation is performed via `validpgpkeys`. This is a standard and acceptable practice in AUR packaging for binary distributions when upstream provides cryptographic signatures.

The `prepare()` function extracts the data.tar.xz from the .deb archive using `tar`, which is a normal step when repackaging a .deb for Arch Linux. The `package()` function copies contents into standard system directories under `$pkgdir` and creates symlinks for the binary, desktop file, and icon—typical post-install setup. All operations are confined to the package build environment and consistent with repackaging a trusted upstream binary.

There are no network requests during build or install, no code execution from remote sources, no obfuscation, and no unexpected file system modifications. The file operations serve the legitimate purpose of installing the Bisq application in a manner consistent with Arch Linux packaging guidelines.
</details>
<summary>
Repackages signed upstream binary; no malicious behavior detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

<details>
The file `net.properties` is a standard Java networking configuration file that sets default values for various network-related system properties. It contains commented and uncommented configuration options for proxy settings, HTTP keep-alive behavior, authentication schemes, header restrictions, and Unix domain socket storage. All settings are typical for a Java runtime environment and align with documented JVM networking properties.

There is no executable code, no network requests, and no file system operations present in this file. The content consists solely of key-value pairs used to configure Java's networking behavior, with safe default values such as disabling system proxy detection (`java.net.useSystemProxies=false`) and restricting weak authentication schemes like Basic over HTTPS tunnels. The only active non-default settings are `http.nonProxyHosts`, `ftp.nonProxyHosts`, and `jdk.net.unixdomain.tmpdir`, all of which define standard exclusions and a temporary directory path.

The configuration restricts rather than enables potentially risky behaviors, such as transparent NTLM authentication (explicitly disabled) and unrestricted HTTP headers (left commented out). The use of `/tmp` for Unix domain sockets is a common default on Unix-like systems. No obfuscation, remote payloads, or suspicious directives are present. This file reflects standard, conservative JVM networking defaults.
</details>
<summary>
Standard Java network config; no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

The file `management.properties` is a standard Java platform management configuration file in `java.util.Properties` format. It defines settings for JMX remote monitoring, SSL/TLS configuration, authentication, access control, and deserialization filtering. All content is well-documented and consistent with Oracle/Sun/OpenJDK JMX agent configurations.

The only active setting is `com.sun.management.jmxremote.serial.filter.pattern`, which configures a deserialization filter to mitigate unsafe object deserialization attacks. This filter explicitly allows known safe classes (e.g., `java.lang.*`, `javax.management.*`) and ends with `!*` to reject any unlisted classes—a secure default.

No code execution, network activity, or file modification is present. Configuration files like this are typically read by the JVM at startup when JMX is enabled. There are no obfuscated commands, unexpected downloads, or exfiltration mechanisms. All settings are optional and intended for administrator use. The file does not enable any insecure defaults by itself—it merely provides commented examples for various configurations.

This file is not part of the AUR packaging logic but likely a bundled configuration for the upstream application (Bisq) when run with JMX monitoring enabled. Its contents pose no supply-chain risk.

It defines cryptographic provider order, algorithm restrictions, SSL/TLS settings, certificate path validations, and other security-related policies. The content is consistent with typical OpenJDK or Oracle JDK distributions.

There are no signs of malicious activity, obfuscated code, unauthorized network calls, or suspicious file operations. All entries are legitimate security directives, such as disabling weak algorithms (e.g., MD5, SHA1, SSLv3), defining preferred providers, configuring certificate distrust policies (e.g., for Symantec, Entrust), and setting secure defaults for cryptographic operations.

While some settings like `security.overridePropertiesFile=true` allow external override via command-line properties, this is standard JVM behavior and not inherently dangerous. Similarly, `policy.allowSystemProperty=true` enables dynamic policy specification, which is expected in development or flexible deployment environments. No remote code execution, telemetry, or data exfiltration mechanisms are present.

The configuration reflects modern security hardening practices, such as enforcing strong algorithms and disabling outdated or insecure ones, aligning with upstream JDK defaults.
